name: Hangzhou West Lake Longjing, Zhejiang (~100m)

staple_foods.name: Longjing green tea (pan-fired)

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 13.99 g/100g dry leaf total catechins (5-catechin HPLC sum: EGC 0.62% + EGCG 8.13% + ECG 3.61% + EC 1.08% + C 0.55% — Zhao et al. 2014 Sci World J 2014:863984, PMC4163330)
key_flavor_bioactives.caffeine_concentration: 3.5-4.5 g/100g dry leaf [Longjing spring tea caffeine ~4.1% per Chinese compositional standard; You et al. 2012 Food Chem 135:2390]

umami_compounds.glutamate: 0.5-1.2 g/100g dry leaf as theanine [lowland green tea; lower than high-altitude due to temperature effect on theanine synthesis]

staple_foods.cultivar: Camellia sinensis var. sinensis, Longjing 43 and traditional West Lake cultivars
staple_foods.notes: Hangzhou West Lake (Xi Hu) Longjing growing area, Zhejiang Province, China. Altitude ~50–200m; centre of production area ~100m. Pan-fired green tea (qing guo process) — no shade, no oxidation, no fermentation. Catechin value (13.99 g/100g) is from Zhao et al. (2014) Scientific World Journal, Table 2: HPLC 5-catechin dry-weight sum for Long jing tea purchased commercially in China. EGCG is the dominant fraction (8.13%). This is the lowest-altitude anchor in the tea altitude regression series, extending the range from 1500–2200m down to ~100m. Longjing is grown on hillside gardens of the West Lake National Tea Park; the West Lake designated-origin zone sits on gently sloping hillsides at 50–200m. Note: commercial sample origin means minor inter-season and inter-grade variation is absorbed into the single point. The value is representative of the Longjing origin and consistent with independent measurements (e.g. 10.1 g/100g for 4 major catechins only, Rietveld & Wiseman 2003 J Nutr). Absent confounds: not shade-grown, not high-altitude, not wild or ancient-tree.

known_processing_methods: pan-fired green tea (qing guo + hui guo)

notes: Hangzhou, Zhejiang Province, eastern China. Sub-tropical humid climate (Köppen Cfa), warm summers, mild winters. West Lake Longjing is China's most prestigious green tea; the protected geographic origin zone covers the hills surrounding Xi Hu at ~50–200m. This RSU provides a low-altitude anchor for the tea catechin vs altitude regression (cf. RSU-20 Darjeeling 2000m, RSU-58 Kericho 2200m). The 100m altitude represents the main West Lake production zone.
data_sources: Zhao CN et al. (2014) — The Galloyl Catechins Contributing to Main Antioxidant Capacity of Tea Made from Camellia sinensis in China. Scientific World Journal 2014:863984. doi:10.1155/2014/863984. PMC4163330.
Rietveld A & Wiseman S (2003) — Antioxidant effects of tea: evidence from human clinical trials. J Nutr 133(10):3275S–3284S.
